findings.issue: The preview of the real-exponent power rule is over-generalized because it omits the domain restriction for arbitrary real exponents.
findings.evidence: The draft says the statement d/dx x^r = r x^(r-1) for an arbitrary real exponent r is true, without saying this is for x > 0 or on the appropriate domain.
findings.suggestion: Qualify the deferred statement, e.g. say it holds for x > 0 when x^r is defined via logarithms, with other domain cases handled later.

summary: One blocking math-domain issue was found. The core seed order and the brief-mandated rules, proofs, examples, e^x derivation, b^x remark, and no-figure/no-strategy constraints otherwise appear satisfied.
